frameworks.name: Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: ideal observer combines prior with likelihood to infer average orientation

frameworks.name: signal-detection / noise decomposition
frameworks.one_line: separates encoding noise from later cognitive integration noise

frameworks.name: near-optimal sensorimotor inference
frameworks.one_line: contrasts intact sensory optimality with suboptimal cognitive judgments

core_question: Why are humans near-optimal in sensory judgments but systematically biased in cognitive ones?
key_assumptions: observers should weight evidence by its reliability under Bayes
two distinct noise sources affect perceptual decisions: encoding and integration
confidence and opt-out behaviour reflect internal uncertainty estimates
manipulations: stimulus contrast (encoding noise) across array of gratings
orientation variability across the array (integration noise)
task demands including base-rate priors, confidence reports, and opt-out option
key_findings: observers adapted near-optimally to changes in encoding noise
under higher integration noise, participants ignored stimulus base rates
they reported excessive confidence and avoided opting out of difficult trials
behaviour was captured by a Bayesian model blind to integration noise
interpretations: the cognitive optimality gap stems from blindness to later integration noise
noise blindness offers a computational account of suboptimal cognitive inference
